coredata.dc:title: Enhancing supercritical carbon dioxide heat transfer in helical tube heat exchangers with twisted tape inserts: A computational investigation
coredata.prism:publicationName: International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer
coredata.prism:copyright: Crown Copyright © 2024 Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Liaw, Kim Leong
Kurnia, Jundika C.
Sasmito, Agus P.
coredata.dc:description: Supercritical carbon dioxide (sCO2) has recently gained considerable attention due to its unique thermal properties that make it favorable as working fluid in various thermal system. It is particularly promising in cooling application of powerplant in combination with helical tube heat exchanger. One way to further enhance its heat transfer performance is by introducing twisted tape insert to the helical tube heat exchanger working with sCO2. Nonetheless, no study evaluating this idea has been reported, withholding further implementation of this innovative idea. Hence, this study aims to investigate the heat transfer performance of sCO2 in helical tubes with twisted tape inserts. A three-dimensional computational model, considering mass, momentum, and energy conservation, is developed and validated against experimental data. The heat transfer characteristics are evaluated in terms of wall and bulk fluid temperature as well as heat transfer coefficient. The impact of twisted tape width, twisting turns, and operating parameters (heat and mass fluxes) are evaluated to gain understanding on the contribution of these parameters on the heat transfer mechanism of the flow. The results reveal that introduction of twisted tape inserts enhanced heat exchange performance because of the intensified secondary flow within the helical tube. The most notable enhancement takes place when the temperature remains within the pseudo-critical region, driven by the higher thermal conductivity that dominates heat transfer near the wall and the higher specific heat capacity, enabling efficient heat absorption while restraining temperature rise. However, increasing the number of turns and tape width does not necessarily result in a greater heat transfer enhancement. Performance evaluation criterion (PEC) is utilized to evaluate overall performance. Further exploration using the Taguchi method underscores the dominance of operating parameters including mass flux and heat flux, over geometric factors like the number of turns and tape width. Moreover, strong interactions are only observed between geometric parameters (number of turns and tape width) and the temperature difference between the inlet and pseudo-critical points. Results from this study are expected to augment critical information required in developing high performance cooling mechanisms for various thermal systems, especially power plants.
coredata.openaccessSponsorName: CAUL institutions 2023: Hybrid journals full funding
coredata.dcterms:subject: Effective thermal system
Potential heat transfer enhancement
Pseudo-critical temperature
Turbulent convective heat transfer
Parameter interaction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematics representation and mesh sample of the investigated straight and helical tube with twisted tape.
Thermal properties of supercritical carbon dioxide at investigated operating pressure of 8.02 MPa.
Mesh independence test outcome in bulk temperature and pressure drop.

Validation of against Wang et al. [26,27] experimental data on pressure drop and outlet temperature of supercritical carbon dioxide at (a) constant inlet temperature and (b) constant mass flux for current developed model.

Validations against experimental result from Zhang et al. [9]: (a) Temperature and (b) Heat transfer coefficient with G = 97.92 kg/m²·s and qwall = 12.13 kW/m²; (c) Temperature and (d) Heat transfer coefficient with G = 206 kg/m²·s and qwall = 20.5 kW/m²; all under inlet pressure, Pin = 8.02 MPa. Dotted line indicates pseudo-critical temperature.

Velocity profile and streamlines of helical tubes: (a) without twisted tape; (b) with twisted tape in base case; (c) with 7 mm wide twisted tape; (d) with 72 turns twisted tape; (e) with twisted tape at 20 kW/m2; and (f) with twisted tape at 200 kg/m2·s.

(a) Local wall temperature (dashed lines) and bulk temperature (solid lines), and (b) heat transfer coefficient, of straight tube, helical tube and different turns of twisted tape inserted helical tubes at operating pressure of 8.02 MPa and wall heat flux of 10 kW/m2. Dotted line at constant temperature indicates pseudo-critical temperature.
Local specific heat capacity and thermal conductivity of base case (Helical tube with 18 turns and 4 mm width twisted tape, operates at 8.02 MPa, 10 kW/m2 and 100 kg/m2·s).

Temperature distribution and heatlines of helical tubes: (a) without twisted tape; (b) with twisted tape in base case; (c) with 7 mm wide twisted tape; (d) with 72 turns twisted tape; (e) with twisted tape at 20 kW/m2; and (f) with twisted tape at 200 kg/m2s.

Results of (a) local wall temperature (dashed lines) and bulk temperature (solid lines), and (b) heat transfer coefficient, of straight tube, helical tube, and helical tubes with 18 turns of different width twisted tape at 8.02 MPa, 10 kW/m2 and 100 kg/m2·s (base case). Dotted line at constant temperature indicates pseudo-critical temperature.

Local wall temperature (dashed lines) and bulk temperature (solid lines) of (a) 15 kW/m2 and (b) 20 kW/m2; and heat transfer coefficient (W/m2·K) of (c) 15 kW/m2 and (d) 20 kW/m2 in different configurations. Dotted line at constant temperature indicates pseudo-critical temperature.

Local wall temperature (dashed lines) and bulk temperature (solid lines) of (a) 150 kg/m2·s and (b) 200 kg/m2·s; and heat transfer coefficient (W/m2·K) of (c) 150 kg/m2·s and (d) 200 kg/m2·s in different configurations. Dotted line at constant temperature indicates pseudo-critical temperature.
Local pressure drop of helical tubes with 18 turns of 4 mm width twisted tape at operating pressure of 8.02 MPa, wall heat flux of 10 kW/m2 at different mass flux.

Performance evaluation criterion (PEC) of helical tube with twisted tape insert for various (a) twisted tape turns, (b) wall heat flux, and (c) inlet mass flux, compared to straight tube without twisted tape insert in respective operating conditions. Tape width of 0 mm indicating helical tube without twisted tape insert.
S/N ratio plot for investigated parameters with respect to (a) Pressure drop, (b) Heat transfer coefficient, (c) Global entropy generation, (d) Performance evaluation criterion.
Interaction plot for investigated parameters with respect to (a) Pressure drop, (b) Heat transfer coefficient, (c) Global entropy generation, (d) Performance evaluation criterion.
Geometrical parameters of the straight and helical tubes as well as twisted tape.
Boundary conditions of the numerical model.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Average heat transfer coefficient (W/m2·K) and pressure drops across tubes (Pa) for helical tubes with various twisted tape at 8.02 MPa, 10 kW/m2 and 100 kg/m2·s.

Average heat transfer coefficient (W/m2·K) and pressure drops across tubes (Pa) of various geometry and configuration at different wall heat flux (kW/m2), 8.02 MPa, 100 kg/m2·s and 18 turns twisted tape insert.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: PEC indicates the performance of the investigated case as compared to straight tube at 10 kW/m2 and 100 kg/m2·s.

head.ce:title: Enhancing supercritical carbon dioxide heat transfer in helical tube heat exchangers with twisted tape inserts: A computational investigation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mining and Materials Engineering, McGill University, 3450 University, Frank Dawson Adams Bldg., Montreal, QC, H3A2A7 Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mining and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering and Science, Curtin University, CDT 250, Miri, Sarawak, 98009, Malaysia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Mechanical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering and Science, Curtin University, CDT 250, Miri, Sarawak, 98009, Malaysia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Supercritical carbon dioxide (sCO2) has recently gained considerable attention due to its unique thermal properties that make it favorable as working fluid in various thermal system. It is particularly promising in cooling application of powerplant in combination with helical tube heat exchanger. One way to further enhance its heat transfer performance is by introducing twisted tape insert to the helical tube heat exchanger working with sCO2. Nonetheless, no study evaluating this idea has been reported, withholding further implementation of this innovative idea. Hence, this study aims to investigate the heat transfer performance of sCO2 in helical tubes with twisted tape inserts. A three-dimensional computational model, considering mass, momentum, and energy conservation, is developed and validated against experimental data. The heat transfer characteristics are evaluated in terms of wall and bulk fluid temperature as well as heat transfer coefficient. The impact of twisted tape width, twisting turns, and operating parameters (heat and mass fluxes) are evaluated to gain understanding on the contribution of these parameters on the heat transfer mechanism of the flow. The results reveal that introduction of twisted tape inserts enhanced heat exchange performance because of the intensified secondary flow within the helical tube. The most notable enhancement takes place when the temperature remains within the pseudo-critical region, driven by the higher thermal conductivity that dominates heat transfer near the wall and the higher specific heat capacity, enabling efficient heat absorption while restraining temperature rise. However, increasing the number of turns and tape width does not necessarily result in a greater heat transfer enhancement. Performance evaluation criterion (PEC) is utilized to evaluate overall performance. Further exploration using the Taguchi method underscores the dominance of operating parameters including mass flux and heat flux, over geometric factors like the number of turns and tape width. Moreover, strong interactions are only observed between geometric parameters (number of turns and tape width) and the temperature difference between the inlet and pseudo-critical points. Results from this study are expected to augment critical information required in developing high performance cooling mechanisms for various thermal systems, especially power plants.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, supercritical carbon dioxide (sCO2) has emerged as a promising working fluid for various thermal engineering application especially on power generation and waste heat recovery systems [1]. This interest is mainly driven by its appealing thermophysical properties such as high specific heat capacity, low specific volume, and low viscosity. Owing to these properties and high operating temperature and pressure, thermal systems using sCO2 generally offer superior thermal efficiency, smaller components, and, as a result, a more compact footprint. Furthermore, CO2 boasts eco-friendly attributes, including non-toxicity, excellent chemical stability, non-flammability, no ozone depletion potential, and a low global warming potential [2]. These will be beneficial for its wide adoption, given the current strong worldwide interest in green technology. In addition to its promising potential for thermal system, drastic changes on the thermophysical properties of supercritical fluids [3], including sCO2, has also attracted considerable attention from material and thermal engineering researcher worldwide. Accordingly, numerous studies investigating the flow dynamics of sCO2 and its associated heat transfer process have been conducted and reported.

Dang and Hihara [4] conducted experimental evaluation of supercritical carbon dioxide heat transfer in horizontal circular tubes with various inner diameter from 1 mm to 6 mm. They suggested that heat transfer coefficient and pressure drop increase with mass flux. Meanwhile the effect of heat flux and tube diameter is affected by other parameters. Cao et al. [5] conducted a similar investigation, studying laminar convective heat transfer in round and triangular miniature tubes having less than 1 mm hydraulic diameter, oriented horizontally. They highlighted the importance of buoyancy in dictating the heat transfer performance particularly adjacent to the pseudo-critical point. The geometrical characteristics of the tube were found to be crucial as well.

Meanwhile, thermal behavior of sCO2 in vertical tube was reported by Jiang et al. [6]. It was reported that the flow direction greatly affects the heat transfer process within the tube. In both directions (downward and upward), buoyancy was found to be instrumental in dictating the heat transfer characteristics. Non-uniform heating effect on the sCO2 heat transfer performance in vertical tube was reported by Fan et al. [7]. It was reported that for considered cases, the significantly higher specific heat capacity in the region close to the tube wall primarily contribute to the enhanced heat transfer while the increase of the viscous sublayer, due to viscosity increase, dominate the deteriorated heat transfer in the tube. Like the previous studies, buoyance is still a prominent factor, especially in the axial direction. While the previously discussed studies dealt with one side of fluid, Cai et al. [8] investigate both by studying the heat exchange between sCO2 and water stream in a microtube heat exchanger. In their study, the evaluated parameters were operating pressure, mass fluxes of CO2 and water. Similar to the previous studies for single fluid, buoyancy significantly influences heat transfer characteristics. It was found that only sCO2 mass flux dictated the heat transfer characteristics while water flux has negligible effect. Water flowing downward was found to have superior heat transfer than water flowing upward in counter flow configuration.

Other than straight tubes, thermal behavior of sCO2 in coiled tube have also been explored and reported. Zhang et al. [9] investigated convective heat transfer of upward-flowing sCO2 in a vertically helically coiled tube. They concluded that heat transfer characteristics of the investigated flow were simultaneously dictated by the key forces (buoyancy and centrifugal forces) and changes in sCO2's physical properties. Similar study was reported by Wang et al. [10] who investigated heat transfer of supercritical CO2 in heated helically coiled tubes. They highlighted the significance of having fluid property as variable rather than set these as constants. In their subsequent study [11], the heat transfer characteristics of sCO2 in the cooled helically coiled tube was evaluated. Several parameters affecting heat transfer were analyzed including operating pressure, heat flux, and mass flux. Increase of heat flux particularly at pseudo-critical temperature region was found to severely impact the heat transfer. On the orientation effect, the helically coiled tube in horizontal orientation has higher heat transfer performance than its vertical counterpart. Li et al. [12] proposed heat transfer enhancement of sCO2 in helically coiled tube by introducing one-sided heating. Enhanced centrifugal force generated by semicircular heating led to a larger velocity gradient in a crescent-shaped flow pattern which in turn resulted in better heat transfer. Yang et al. [13] evaluated the effect of non-circular cross-section on the heat transfer performance of sCO2 in helically coiled tube. It was found that increase of polygon edge number leads to increase in heat transfer coefficient at the cost of higher pressure drop. It was emphasized that the increase range gradually decreases as the number of polygon edge increases.

Twisting flow geometry impact on the thermal behavior of sCO2 has also been investigated. Zhao et al. [14] investigated flow characteristic and heat transfer of sCO2 in a twisted straight tube heat exchanger. It was found that the performance of twisted tube is superior to that of straight tube by up to 1.96 times. Li et al. [15] numerically evaluated potential enhancement of sCO2 heat transfer in a straight tube by incorporating twisted tape insert. It was reported that up to 2- or 3-times better heat transfer can be attained by implementing twisted tape insert. Comparable investigation was reported by Cong et al. [16]. They investigated the effect twisted tape implementation in mitigating heat transfer deterioration of sCO2 in straight tube. The introduction of twisted tape weakened the buoyancy effect and positively contributed to suppressing heat transfer deterioration. Liu et al. [17] reported that a novel alternative elliptical twisted tube offer better flow and heat transfer performance. An enhancement in heat transfer performance by up to 107 % and 116 % for horizontal and vertical orientation were reported, respectively. Meng et al. [18] introduced a static mixers with three helical blades to enhance turbulent convective heat transfer performance of sCO2 in straight tube. Higher heat transfer performance was obtained by using the proposed insert.

As can be inferred from the previous studies, geometrical properties of the tubes/heat exchanger play a significant role in dictating the flow behavior and the associated heat transfer of sCO2 with notable influence on the thermophysical properties that directly determine the heat transfer performance. In our previous study dealing with turbulent convective heat transfer of water in straight and helical tubes [19], the helical coiled tube with twisted tape was found to offer the best heat transfer performance. Swirl generating heat exchanger shows potential in further enhancement by utilizing the desired properties for heat transfer in sCO2. Thus, combining helical tube with twisted tape insert may lead to better heat transfer performance of sCO2 which may provide insightful knowledge in managing effectiveness of thermal system with sCO2. From the literature review, however, no study investigating turbulent flow and its associated convective heat transfer of sCO2 in helically coiled tube with twisted tape insert has been reported. Therefore, the primary aim of this study is to investigate the turbulent flow and heat transfer characteristic of sCO2 in helically coiled tube with twisted tape insert. To have a comprehensive view, the sensitivity of several key parameters expected to affect heat transfer performance including inlet mass flux, wall heat flux, twisted tape turn and width are evaluated. The overall performance of the helical tubes with twisted tape insert will be compared against helical tube without insert and straight tube to evaluate the potential heat transfer enhancement offered.

The numerical model of helically coiled tubes equipped with twisted tape insert is prepared according to the fundamental conservation principles and turbulent model combined with supercritical properties of CO2. Fig. 1 shows the geometries of the investigated configurations and the mesh sample used in the computational study. Details on the considered geometry parameters are tabulated in Table 1 . The helical tube geometry is built according to physical helical tube utilized by Zhang et al. [9]. Twisted tape is designed to be thin so that it has minimal influence on the volume in the helical tube while retaining the ability to disturb the flow in the helical tube [20]. The material of the twisted tape is steel with density of 8030 kg/m3, specific heat of 502.48 J/kg·K, and thermal conductivity of 16.27 W/m·K. Heat flux is supplied to heat up the developed model in order to simulate real-life cooling of equipment with designed heat exchanger.

Supercritical fluid is an interesting state of fluid that occurs when it is subjected to pressure and temperature beyond its critical point. In its supercritical condition, CO2 exhibits a density similar to its liquid state, but low viscosity close its gas state. These unique properties offer superior flow and heat transfer than traditional heat transfer fluid. The properties of supercritical fluid change drastically near the critical temperature. In this study, the CO2 is subjected to operating pressure of 8.02 MPa in which the pseudo-critical temperature is around 308 K. The reason for choosing this particular pressure is because of the interesting spike of properties near to the critical temperature at this pressure (Fig. 2 ), which will diminish at higher pressure. In this condition, the properties of the working fluid, sCO2, are defined in piecewise linear according to value obtained from Reference Fluid Properties (REFPROP) from National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) [21]. The properties of the working fluid are shown in Fig. 2. The inlet temperature, Tin , is set at 288 K to make sure that the changes of the properties are experienced in the model.

The simultaneous mass, momentum and heat transfer processes occurring within the investigated heat exchanger tube is taken into account by conservation equation of mass, momentum, and energy of the supercritical CO2, as presented in Eqs. (1)-(3). While Eq. (4) is the energy equation of solid.
(1)
∇ ·

u = 0
(2)

= − ∇ · p I + ∇ ·

c
p , f

e f f

τ ¯

(4)

= 0

In addition, the Shear Stress Transport (SST) k-ω (omega) turbulence model that directly resolves the viscous sublayer with fine mesh at dimensionless wall distance (y +) approximately 1 is implemented to the model. This turbulence model (Eqs. (5) and (6) is chosen due to its superior accuracy among the turbulence model available in predicting fluid flow with swirl generator [22], which is the case considered in this study.
(5)

= ∇ ·

One of the important characteristics of the mentioned turbulence model is the turbulent viscosity in Eq. (7).
(7)

In the above equations, the symbol
τ ¯
denotes stress tensor,
ρ f

is gravitational body force, F is external body forces,
σ K
and

are turbulent Prandtl numbers,
G K
is production of turbulence kinetic energy,
G ω
is generation of specific dissipation rate,
Y K
is dissipation of turbulence kinetic energy due to turbulence,
Y ω
is dissipation of specific turbulence dissipation rate due to turbulence,
D ω
is cross-diffusion term,
G b
is buoyancy generated turbulence and
G
ω b
is buoyancy term in specific turbulence dissipation equation.

The heatline is computed and illustrated to show the heat transfer in the fluid, similar to streamline for fluid flow [23]. The function of heatline is given by:
(8)

(9)

∂ T

The computational model is bounded by the defined boundary conditions as listed in Table 2 .

To evaluate the overall heat transfer performance of the investigated geometry combinations in different operational conditions, performance evaluation criterion (PEC) is employed [24], and it is given as in Eq. (10).
(10)
P E C =
(
N u
N
u
s t r a i g h t
)
(
f
f
s t r a i g h t
)
1 3

Through this, the performance of each particular case can be evaluated with consideration of heat transfer and fluid flow in constant pumping work condition. Subscript “straight” indicates the Nusselt number or friction factor of conventional straight tube without twisted tape at that particular working condition, which act as the most basic heat exchanger. Therefore, PEC of straight tube is always equal to 1. Nusselt number, Nu, and friction factor, f, of each case are computed from bulk temperatures and properties according to Liao and Zhao [25].
(11)
N u =
h b
d
k b
(12)
f =
Δ
p d
1 2
ρ b
u b
2
L
In here, h is the heat transfer coefficient, k is thermal conductivity, Δp is the pressure drop across the tube, ρ is density, u is velocity and subscript ‘b’ indicates bulk.

In realizing numerical simulation and prediction, the structure of helical tube and the twisted tapes are constructed through ANSYS Design Modeller, meshed in ANSYS Meshing and computed in ANSYS Fluent, where the governing equations, turbulence model, materials thermophysical properties and boundary conditions are set up with the computational domain mentioned. The incorporated solution method for pressure-velocity coupling scheme is coupled solver. Least squares cell based is used to solve spatial discretization of gradient, first order upwind for turbulent kinetic energy and specific dissipation rate (checked for current case with maximum 2 % difference to second order upwind), and second order upwind for pressure, momentum, and energy. The pseudo transient method is included in adjustment of relaxation factors. Residual criteria for convergence are checked at 1e−6. A typical simulation required 3000–5000 iterations and roughly 4–6 h to accomplish the convergence requirements using 12 core processors with 4 GB RAM allocation for each core.

To confirm the amount of mesh is out of the factor in the accuracy of numerical model prediction, the results from the coarsest mesh (242,688) to the finest mesh (5,129,600) is recorded and plotted in Fig. 3 . All results are relatively compared to the finest mesh available to seek balance between prediction consistency and computational power requirement due to the increase number of mesh. Mesh configuration with 2,198,400 mesh elements was found to have 0.02 % relative difference in heat transfer coefficient and 0.26 % relative difference in pressure drop, indicating good consistency with the finest mesh results while demanding acceptable computational resources. Therefore, this mesh configuration is implemented for all cases in this study.

The numerical investigations on supercritical CO2 in helically coiled tube with twisted tape is conducted at 8.02 MPa. The corresponding pseudo-critical temperature of this considered pressure is 308 K. The impacts of several key parameters such as tube geometry (straight and helical), twisted tape geometry (number of turn and width) and operating parameters (mass flux, heat flux and inlet temperature) will be numerically assessed. Helical tube of 4 mm width and 18 turns twisted tape, 10 kW/m2 wall heat flux and 100 kg/m2·s mass flux is set as the base case unless mentioned otherwise.

In order to execute reliable evaluations of sCO2 in helical tubes with twisted tape numerically, the developed computational model needed to be validated to ensure accurate prediction that aligns with real-life phenomena. For this purpose, two validations have been conducted. The first validation is carried out to check the pressure drop behavior of sCO2 under heating conditions. Experimental data is obtained from publication by Wang et al. [26,27], which studied sCO2 in uniformly heated straight tube. The particular case and operating ranges that was used to evaluate present model are 1.0 mm diameter tube, pressure at 8.05 MPa, mass flux of 672 – 1202 kg/m2·s, wall heat flux of 124 – 170 kW/m2 and inlet temperature of 304 – 310 K. The form loss due to the sudden change of cross section at the adiabatic connection at inlet and outlet in their experiment is estimated and omitted from their total pressure drop as it is not modelled for validation and not of interest in current study. The results of the validations are depicted in Fig. 4 below. The pressure difference obtained from present numerical results are within 5 % of the sum of frictional pressure drop and accelerational pressure drop in Fig. 4(a), while the pressure drop is less than 10 % in Fig. 4(b), with various inlet temperature. The temperature outlet data available is also used to validate the present study's model as well. Minor differences indicate that the current setup of computational model is able to realize physical heat transfer, fluid flow, and fluid thermophysical properties with meaningful outcome.
Second, experimental data of supercritical CO2 in helical tube by Zhang et al. [9] is extracted and compared with our numerical prediction as presented in Fig. 5 .

In this validation exercise, the model geometry and operating parameters are identical to those presented by Zhang et al. [9]. The error bar presented in the figure indicates 5 % deviation of the experimental data. It is presented to assess quick evaluation on the accuracy of the developed model. As can be seen, the temperature deviation of numerical results as compared to the experimental data is about 1 % at low heat flux and mass flux as shown in Fig. 5(a) and less than 0.5 % at high heat flux and mass flux as shown in Fig. 5(c). The simulated heat transfer coefficient in Fig. 5(b) closely follows the same trend as the experimental results, with a relative difference of approximately 9.7 %, which falls within the uncertainty range as specified in Zhang's paper (±20 %). For higher mass flux and heat flux conditions, as shown in Fig. 5(d), the average deviation between numerical predictions and experimental data is approximately 5 %. This validation indicates the accuracy and reliability of the developed model in predicting heat transfer phenomena of the investigated supercritical CO2.

Flow behavior and heat transfer mechanisms are profoundly influenced by flow geometry. The intricate relationship of flow characteristics and convective heat transfer within a system are dictated by the geometry of the flow passage and thermophysical properties of the heat transfer fluid. Consequently, evaluating the impact of flow passage geometry is paramount to elaborate the heat transfer mechanism and identify potential heat transfer performance enhancement. In this study, the flow passage varies from straight to helical tube. In addition, various types of twisted tape inserts (with different number of turns and tape width) are introduced to the helical tube to enhance its heat transfer performance. Their impacts on the heat transfer performance are evaluated and discussed in the following subsection.

Here, the impact of adding twisted tape insert to helical tube is evaluated. Cabeza et al. [28] stated that increasing the turbulence can significantly reduce heat transfer deterioration. While the curvature of the helical tube naturally generates secondary flows through the combined influence of centrifugal and buoyancy forces, the introduction of a twisted tape insert serves to further intensify these secondary flows by acting as a swirl generator that disrupting the flow. The intensification of radial direction flows can be noticed in comparing Fig. 6 (a) and (b), as the streamlines distorted with the presence of tape in the middle of tube. The velocity magnitude is also slightly larger when the twisted tape is inserted.

For heat transfer, the local temperatures and heat transfer coefficients are plotted in Fig. 7 . While the temperatures of the bulk fluid are very similar, the highest wall temperature is recorded by the straight tube and closely followed by helical tube without tape, indicating lower cooling performance as compared to those with twisted tape insert which yielded lower wall temperature. This is because heat energy is accumulated at the wall instead of transferring into the fluid. The temperatures near the critical temperature region are having a clear flattening trend due to significant increase of specific heat by up to 10 times. As the temperature departs above the critical point, the specific heat decreases, and the increase of temperature accelerates under constant heat flux. The positive impact of twisted tape insert addition to the performance of helical tube heat transfer is also reaffirmed in Fig. 7(b), where the heat transfer coefficient of helical tube without tape (average of 877.87 W/m2·K) is lower than its twisted tape embedded counterpart, by about 100 W/m2·K. The most significant difference can be observed in the pseudo-critical region. The smooth straight tube and helical tube have a peak heat transfer coefficient of 1268.75 W/m2·K and 1406.15 W/m2·K, respectively, which makes them obviously lower than the twisted taped inserted helical tube that recorded 1705.51 W/m2·K and above. This is probably due to effective removal of local supercritical fluid (fluid with temperature higher than critical temperature, which has lower viscosity) from the wall to have sub-critical fluid takeover the heat transfer near the wall with colder temperature (higher thermal conductivity).

The local and bulk heat conductivity and specific heat capacity in Fig. 8 also explains the reason of peak heat transfer coefficient location at prior of critical temperature. As the wall temperature achieves critical temperature earlier in the flow, the local thermal conductivity near the wall declines earlier than the bulk thermal conductivity. Since the heat transfer occurs at the wall, that is why the heat transfer coefficient is higher before the bulk temperature reaches critical temperature. Moreover, the expansion of specific heat capacity is attributed to the maximum heat transfer coefficient immediately preceding the critical point of the bulk temperature due to the same reason as thermal conductivity. Similar conclusion can be applied for viscosity for its thermophysical nature, where lower viscosity at higher temperature will result in lean viscous boundary layer for free stream fluid with bulk temperature to approach wall. Additionally, it can be seen in Fig. 9 (a) and (b) that the heat transfers across the radial direction are more aggressive with twisted tape, as indicated by the heatlines. It shows that the convective heat transfer in the fluid increases, so that the temperature distribution is more even, and the temperature gradient is high for heat energy to transfer from the wall to the fluid.

Further detail in examining the twisted tape affection towards wall temperature in Fig. 7(a), the increase of twist turns among the helical tubes with twisted tape do not reflect notable impact to the temperature. But it is interesting to note that the helical tube with lowest turns of twisted tape has lower wall temperature occasionally. Lookup in Fig. 5(b) shows that the radial flow in the base case with 18 turns twisted tape is having streamline vortex near the tube wall, implying that high fluid mixing near tube wall. The intense fluid activity near the wall affects the heat transfer in the fluid (Fig. 9b) conform with the heatlines, therefore the wall temperature is experiencing lower temperature as the vortex approaching. On the other hand, despite having two vortexes and higher intensity for 72 turns twisted tape helical tube (Fig. 6d), they are located near to the twisted tape. Therefore, the wall temperature is more even with less turbulence.

Pressure drops across the tubes are tabulated in Table 3 . The increment in pressure drop is expected, due to the increase of flow disturbance frequency corresponding to the number of turns. Meanwhile, the average heat transfer coefficients of twisted tape inserted tubes are found to be 1009.5 W/m2·K, 967.8 W/m2·K and 964.9 W/m2·K, for 18 turns, 36 turns and 72 turns, respectively. Further observation into Fig. 7(b), the fluctuation cascaded from wall temperature are magnified in heat transfer coefficients. All of the helical tubes with twisted tape show fluctuations, with the amplitude of 18 turns being the highest. 18 turns twisted tapes obtain the highest heat transfer coefficient thanks to the fluctuations. The fluctuation of temperature and heat transfer rate in helical tube with twisted tape have also been reported by Liaw et al. [19] and Sun et al. [29] in their studies investigating the integration of multiple swirl flow generation tools. This phenomenon happens probably because the disturbance of the flow by the twisted tape and helical tube do not complement each other all the time, rather having a periodic contraction [30] according to their own cycle. This is evidenced by different fluctuation trends of twisted tape with different number of turns. This further suggests that more turns of twisted tape at the middle of the tube (with 4 mm width) do not create desired constructive turbulence for maximizing heat transfer rate when twisted tape are partnering with helical tube in creating turbulences in the flow. It should be noted that they still enhance the heat transfer rate as compared to straight tube and helical tubes without tape, which inferred that the continuous disturbance of the flow by the helical tube geometry and twisted tape are able to enhance heat transfer.

As extension of twisted tape width will increase the disturbance towards the wall, it is of interest to assess the influence of twisted tape width in the fluid flow characteristics and heat transfer phenomena. Fig. 10 depicted the wall temperature, bulk temperature, and heat transfer coefficient of helical tube with various width of twisted tape insert. Looking at the temperature profile along the tube, the effect of twisted tape width expansion is not significant. The fluctuation of wall temperature is happening to investigated twisted tape width, with more streamline and heatline vortexes near the wall (Figs. 6c and 9c). The path of the fluid and heat flow are longer because of more restriction with 7 mm width tape across the radial direction. Through observing the heat transfer coefficient in Fig. 10(b), it is found that the widest twisted tape records the maximum average heat transfer coefficient (1024.7 W/m2·K), although the improvement is marginal compared to 6 mm (1019.0 W/m2·K), 5 mm (1018.4 W/m2·K), and 4 mm (1009.5 W/m2·K). This proves the elongation of tape width indeed increases the turbulence hence improve heat transfer rate, albeit slightly. Addition to the observation of heat transfer coefficient fluctuations in cases with twisted tape, increasing of tape width do not change the fluctuating pattern, meaning the pattern is dominated by the twisted tape turns and probably helical tube geometry. More discussions on the combined effect of twisted tape width and heat flux will be discussed in the next sub section.

Conversely, the twisted tape with 36 and 72 turns which are considered as obstruction or a bad combination with helical tubes presented decreasing average heat transfer coefficient pattern as the width increases (Table 3). This shows that, as twisted tape is dominating the flow by expanding width, the outcome is becoming worse, given their high number of turns. The effect of wide twisted tape to the flow is also echoed in the results for pressure drop, where the pressure drop increases as the width increases. The penalty of pressure drop is further amplified by the number of turns, making helical tube with 7 mm 72 turns twisted tape to have pressure drop across the tube as high as 542.8 Pa. It is close to 50 % pressure drop increment from 4 mm 18 turns twisted tape case.

In industrial applications, it is a common occurrence that the heat flux that needs to be dissipated varies based on the output demand of the equipment. Therefore, wall heat flux values of 10 kW/m2, 15 kW/m2 and 20 kW/m2 are imposed to helical tubes with 18 turns of 4mm-width twisted tape to investigate its impact on the heat transfer process. Referred to Figs. 10(a), 11 (a) and (b), the required tube length for bulk temperature to reach critical temperature has been brought closer to the inlet as the heat flux increases. At heat flux of 20 kW/m2, the wall temperature exceeds critical temperature immediately after going through inlet. The length of the tube that has bulk temperature stays on the critical temperature decreases with the increase of the heat flux. Higher heat flux raises the temperature to supercritical rapidly, reducing the chances of heat energy to transfer into the fluid when they are having desired thermophysical properties for heat transfer before (high density, thermal conductivity, and viscosity) and during (high specific heat capacity) the critical temperature. The temperature increase in supercritical temperature is proportional to the length of the tube.

The corresponding heat transfer coefficient are shown in Figs. 10(b), 11(c) and (d). The peak heat transfer coefficient decreases as the heat flux increases. Besides, it can be clearly seen that due to rapid increase of temperature, the portion of high heat transfer coefficient during superior thermophysical properties in sub-critical region is reduced, while the portion of low heat transfer rate after the critical temperature elongate. The outlet temperature increases, but in overall, the average heat transfer coefficient decreases as shown in Table 4 . The results are concurrent to review summary of Xie et al. [31]. Moreover, the thermophysical properties of sCO2 at pseudo-critical temperature combining with swirl flow effect from twisted tape and helical tube contribute uncertainty in the trend of heat transfer coefficient. In the case where wall heat flux is high and the pseudo-critical section of the tube is short (i.e., 20 kW/m2), the impact of twisted tape width becomes more obvious that narrower width is actually increasing the heat transfer rate. While for low wall heat flux of 10 kW/m2, wider twisted tape is more valuable in creating a higher heat transfer rate within the longer tube section that is close to pseudo-critical temperature. The fact that the troughs of the heat transfer fluctuations always go back to the identical rate in each cycle despite tape width suggesting those are the points or regions where the interference of flows destroy each other. The counter interferences of swirl flow rendered the heat transfer rate behavior similar to helical tube without tape, as the troughs of the fluctuating flow is touching the heat transfer rate of helical tube as in Fig. 11.

Contours comparison of (b) and (e) in Figs. 6 and 9 depicted that the temperature of 20 kW/m2 at same position is higher than 10 kW/m2 case, and the velocity is faster proportional to the temperature despite having the same mass flux input. This inferred that the heat transfer before the critical temperature is more crucial in supercritical CO2 heat transfer than the convective heat transfer after the critical temperature. The extension of bulk and wall temperature in pseudo-critical temperature region will effectively increase the heat transfer coefficient.

Furthermore, Table 4 shows that the heat transfer improvement achieved by a helical tube without tape compared to a straight tube is relatively low. Integration of a helical tube with twisted tape yields a more substantial enhancement in heat transfer. Additionally, Table 4 indicated that the width of the twisted tape has a minimal impact on heat transfer. As for the pressure drop, the increase of pressure drop in high wall heat flux is probably due to the decrease in density which results in higher velocity, therefore, produce higher friction.

Fig. 6(b) and (f) velocity contours between highest and lowest mass flux shows interesting results, where 200 kg/m2·s mass flux is having higher velocity at the cross section closer to the inlet but lower velocity in the cross section near to the outlet of the tube. This is attributed to the lower temperature in the high mass flux scenario (Fig. 9f), that results in higher density and viscosity. Due to this observation, the local pressure drop of each different mass flux is depicted in Fig. 13 . It can be seen that in case with 100 kg/m2·s, the pressure drops decrease after bulk fluid exceeding critical temperature. This is because the viscosity in supercritical temperature is very low as compared to sub critical temperature. Whereas the case of 200 kg/m2·s did not have its fluid at low viscosity with its bulk temperature. The increase in pressure drop is a result of increased mass flux.

The overall performances of sCO2 in helical tube and twisted tape configurations comprise of friction factor and Nusselt number of each case are assessed by using Eq. (10). All cases are compared to straight tube with no twisted tape corresponding to identical operating conditions. The straight tubes acted as the base cases are always having PEC equal to 1, differentiating performance enhancement (PEC > 1) and deterioration (PEC < 1). Detailed PEC for each case is shown in Tables 3, 4, and 5. In Fig. 14 (a), it can be seen that 18 turns twisted tape in helical tubes enhance the heat transfer performance at 4 mm (PEC = 1.04) and 5 mm (PEC = 1.02) tape width, while increase of tape turns decline the performance due to reduction of heat transfer and increase of pressure drop as discussed in Section 4.2. Bad combination of 72 turns, 6 to 7 mm width twisted tape in helical tubes yield PEC of about 0.84 only.

In alternating wall heat flux, it is presented in Fig. 14(b) that the maximum overall performance is at 1.13 at 20 kW/m2 wall heat flux. As investigated in Section 4.3, the heat transfer coefficient is low at high wall heat flux, however, the relative enhancement of helical tube is higher in cases of 20 kW/m2. Together with low bulk thermal conductivity, the Nusselt number increases the PEC. Also, it should be noted that the temperature range of the helical tube heat exchanger can be up to more than 200 K, which is a large difference that may impose undesired fatigue in equipment and materials.

On the other hand, increasing inlet mass flux happened to obtain massive heat transfer rate, as shown in Section 4.4, but increasing the flow velocity imposes higher pressure difference to drive the flow. Moreover, higher viscosity and density of sCO2 at low temperatures further amplify the pressure drop across the tube, therefore resulting slightly lower PEC as compared to cases with high wall heat fluxes. The advantage of high mass flux is the ability to transfer high heat transfer rate at narrow temperatures range. Due to low temperature differences, the impact of thermophysical properties diminished while forced convection dominates the performance. It is proven in Fig. 14(c), where helical tube without twisted tape insert has the highest PEC (1.10), while the tape inserted adds pressure drop to the flow.

In the previous section, the effect of key parameters on the flow characteristics and corresponding heat transfer has been evaluated and discussed. In real life application, multiple key parameters may change simultaneously. Thus, it is imperative to evaluate the significance of individual parameter as well as impact of simultaneous change of several parameters on the heat transfer performance and identify the constructive interaction of key parameter combination to achieve the objectives (minimum pressure drop, minimum entropy generation, maximum heat transfer coefficient and maximum PEC). For this purpose, a simple yet robust and intuitive Taguchi statistical method is employed. Widely adopted in various industries to improve product quality and process performance, this method offers capability to evaluate the interaction between each parameter and their effects on the output and the determination of the best combination of variables for the desired output [32,33]. In this study, 5 parameters with 3 level each are evaluated as shown in Table 6 . By brute force, to investigate every possible combination, 243 numerical simulations will be needed. By adopting Taguchi method, it is possible to achieve it by only having 27 simulations, as illustrated in Table 7 .

To evaluate the effect of investigated parameters, focus will be given to 3 desired output parameters, i.e., pressure drop, heat transfer coefficient, PEC and entropy generation. The significance of each investigated parameter to the outputs is presented and discussed in terms of signal-to-noise (S/N) ratio. For pressure drop and entropy generation, minimum value is preferred, thus S/N ratio smaller is better is selected. In contrast, higher heat transfer coefficient and PEC are desired for heat transfer process, therefore S/N ratio smaller is better is chosen. Both ratios are defined as follows [34]
(13)
S / N
(
smaller is better
)
= − 10 log
(
1 n
∑
h
2
)
(14)
S / N
(
larger is better
)
= − 10 log
(
1 n
∑
1
h
2
)
where n is the number of design parameter and h is the concerned result.

The Taguchi orthogonal array results are presented together with the investigated combination of parameters in Table 7. The S/N ratio plot together with the interaction plot for the investigated parameters are depicted in Figs. 15 and 16 . Based on the presented S/N plots, it is proved that operating parameters (mass flux and heat flux) exert a more dominant influence than geometric parameters like the number of turns and the width of the twisted tape insert. Upon further observation, it becomes apparent that to minimize pressure drop, all parameters should be set to their minimum values, except for the temperature difference between the inlet and the pseudo-critical point. Assessing the slope of the lines, it is apparent that inlet mass flux is the dominant factor influencing the imposed pressure drop in the tube. This is expected as higher mass flux corresponds to higher velocity which is directly proportional to the friction within the tube. As for the wall heat flux, the increase of pressure drop due to higher heat flux is most likely attributed to the smaller density which induces higher velocity of the flow and thus higher friction. Comparing the effect of twisted tape width and number of turns, it is clear that the former has a more pronounced effect on the pressure drop. This is rather interesting since a greater number of turns is expected to significantly increase the swirl motion and thus friction inside the tube. However, its effect is weaker as compared to the increase of friction due to the larger surface area of the wider tape.

Meanwhile, to attain a higher Heat transfer coefficient (HTC), it is advisable to decrease the number of turns and the width of the twisted tape insert while maintaining a low wall heat flux (Fig. 15b). Simultaneously, it is beneficial to increase the inlet mass flux and the temperature difference between the inlet and pseudo-critical temperatures. Comparing all parameters, again, mass flux is clearly the dictating factor among them followed by wall heat flux. Detailed discussion on the effect of these parameters has been elaborated in the previous section.

Because heat transfer dominates entropy generation, as evidenced by the unity Bejan number in all investigated cases, the influence of the parameters under investigation on entropy generation closely mirrors their impact on heat transfer coefficient (HTC) in Fig. 15(c). The only exception is the temperature difference between the inlet and the pseudo-critical point which indicates smaller difference is advantageous for minimizing entropy generation within the tube. The only parameter increase that proves beneficial in minimizing entropy generation within the tube is the inlet mass flux. This outcome aligns with expectations, as higher mass flux leads to a reduced temperature gradient between the wall and the bulk fluid. Consequently, entropy generation due to heat transfer is smaller compared to cases with lower mass flux. Although higher mass flux results in increased velocity and, therefore, higher entropy generation due to friction, it is important to note that the contribution of the friction component is marginal, as indicated by the unity Bejan number.

PECs in this section are all compared to straight tube at 10 kW/m2 and 100 kg/m2·s to include effect of operating parameters in overall performance evaluation. Through examination of S/N ratio plot of PEC (Fig. 15d), it is found that the role of tape turn and tape width become significant when both pressure drop and heat transfer are evaluated together. The interpretation of the graph indicates that less tape turns, and tape width can improve overall performance whereas higher mass flux will probably result in better performance. Wall heat flux, even though has strong effect as presented in S/N ratio plot, becomes complicated as higher wall heat flux benefits pressure drop while low wall heat flux yields better heat transfer. Inlet temperature difference is also presented to be unclear in the trend, however, it is relatively insignificant as compared to other parameters.

Examining the interaction plot in Fig. 16, it is clear that geometric parameters (the number of turns and the width of the twisted tape insert) exhibit strong interactions with the temperature difference between the inlet and the pseudo-critical point, influencing key output parameters such as pressure drop, heat transfer coefficient, entropy generation and overall performance. Conversely, there are weaker interactions observed between the operating parameters, i.e., inlet mass flux and wall heat flux, and these three parameters. For pressure drop, number of turns demonstrate some interaction with inlet mass flux, indicated by intersection of line. Looking back at Table 7, it is interesting to note that helical tube with the smallest number of turn and narrowest twisted tape insert not only scores higher heat transfer coefficient but also smallest pressure drop among the considered cases. It is also the configuration that generates less amount of entropy within the tube. Following the observations of increase effect of tape in PEC S/N ratio plot, the interactions of those geometry parameters are more engaged to mass flux and heat flux in Fig. 16(d). This shows PEC is sensitive to all of the parameters, thus requiring attention to find balances in all of the parameters.

This study employs computational fluid dynamics (CFD) to numerically investigate the turbulent convective heat transfer of supercritical carbon dioxide in helical tubes with twisted tape inserts. Model predictions on the wall and bulk temperature along the helical tube together with the corresponding heat transfer coefficient are compared to the corresponding experimental data to assess the accuracy and validity of the model, for which good agreement was obtained. To better comprehend heat transfer mechanism of the flow in the tube, the effect of twisted tape width, twisting ratio, and operating parameters (heat and mass fluxes) were evaluated. The results shed some insight where introduction of twisted tape insert does increase heat transfer performance by intensifying secondary flow that is present in helical tube. The most pronounced enhancement is recorded by keeping the temperature of bulk fluid smaller and close to the pseudo-critical region, which make full use of the high thermal conductivity and specific heat capacity before they drop in supercritical temperature. It should be noted, however, that increasing the number of turns and width of the twisted tape does not necessarily increase the heat transfer enhancement. Performance evaluation criterion (PEC) analysis shows enhancement in narrow tapes (4 and 5 mm), high heat flux and high mass flux. However, operational temperature range should be considered in application.

Further evaluating the simultaneous impact of key parameters by adopting Taguchi method reveal that operating parameters, including inlet mass flux and wall heat flux, are more dominant factor than geometric parameters like the number of turns and the width of the twisted tape insert. Increasing tape turn and width severely affects all output parameters (pressure drop, heat transfer coefficient and entropy generation) while increasing mass flux positively impacts heat transfer and entropy generation but not the pressure drop. Increasing wall heat flux produces similar effects to those of tape geometries albeit with more severe impact. Interestingly, the increase in inlet and pseudo-critical temperature difference has a positive impact on both heat transfer and pressure drop but not in entropy generation. Furthermore, tape width and tape turn become significant in evaluating their sensitivity in PEC, and the interaction between parameters are stronger in their impact on overall performance. Lastly, strong interaction is observed between geometric parameters (the number of turns and the width of the twisted tape insert) and the inlet and pseudo-critical temperature difference.

Bejan number
(
=
S ˙
h
S ˙
h
+
S ˙
μ
)
Entropy generation due to heat transfer (W/K)

ce:section.ce:para: Kim Leong Liaw: Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Jundika C. Kurnia: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Agus P. Sasmito: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
